physiologicalDescription: Left-sided body symptoms can come from muscle strain, posture, nerve compression, sciatica, neck/back problems, joint issues, circulation problems, heart attack symptoms, stroke/TIA symptoms, lung/spleen/kidney/colon issues, injury, shingles, inflammatory disease, or referred pain from internal organs.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not call sudden left-sided weakness, numbness, chest pain, or face droop an energy blockage. That is how people end up arguing with emergency medicine from the wrong side of time.
mindfulnessPrompts: Is this sudden or severe?
Are there stroke or heart symptoms?
What physical system could refer pain here?
Where do I need support instead of one-sided carrying?
physicalTherapyTip: Track exact location, onset, sudden vs gradual, weakness, numbness, face droop, speech/vision/balance changes, chest pressure, shortness of breath, jaw/arm/back pain, abdominal pain, fever, rash, injury, and neurologic symptoms.

name: Lung Problems
emotionalRoot: Depression, deep unexpressed grief, sorrow, and a fear of taking in life fully. Feeling like you do not deserve to live life to the fullest or take up space in the world.
metaphor: Your lung bellows collapsing under the heavy, suffocating blanket of old grief that you refuse to exhale and let go of.
physiologicalDescription: Sustained emotional sorrow and depressive states depress deep breathing, restricting alveolar ventilation and altering the balance of autonomic tone, making the lungs highly vulnerable to chronic congestion.
sarcasticAdvice: Magnificent. Keep holding your breath and shallow-breathing like a scared rabbit to make sure you don't take up too much space. I'm sure that carrying the entire world's unexpressed sorrow is a fantastic way to keep your respiratory tissue healthy.
mindfulnessPrompts: What deep grief or heavy sadness am I carrying in my chest that I have never allowed myself to fully cry out?
Do I believe I have a fundamental right to breathe freely, take up space, and enjoy my life?
physicalTherapyTip: Practice gentle, slow chest expansion stretches. Inhale slowly for 4 seconds, expand your ribs wide, hold for 4 seconds, and exhale completely for 6 seconds to clear stagnant alveolar air.

name: Menopause Problems
emotionalRoot: Transition, identity change, grief, heat, aging anxiety, changing roles, and the body demanding a new operating manual.
metaphor: Your body is a building with a confused thermostat, retiring hormone managers, and occupants trying to survive heat and AC at the same time.
physiologicalDescription: Menopause is defined after 12 months without a menstrual period and is related to declining ovarian hormone production. Perimenopause can cause irregular cycles, hot flashes, night sweats, sleep problems, mood changes, brain fog, vaginal dryness, urinary symptoms, libido changes, joint pain, and bone/cardiovascular risk changes.
sarcasticAdvice: Hot flashes are not a personality defect. Your hypothalamus is just running the HVAC system like it got trained by a raccoon.
mindfulnessPrompts: What identity is changing?
What am I grieving?
What support do I need for this transition?
What symptom needs medical care instead of endurance?
physicalTherapyTip: Track bleeding pattern, hot flashes, sleep, mood, vaginal/urinary symptoms, libido, joint pain, migraines, medications, and risk factors. Seek care for postmenopausal bleeding, heavy bleeding, severe depression/anxiety, self-harm thoughts, chest pain, or new neurologic symptoms.

name: Migraine Headache
emotionalRoot: Resisting the flow of life, extreme perfectionism, intense self-control and suppression of emotions, or punishing yourself for not being flawless in your efforts.
metaphor: Your cranial blood vessels staging a violent, throbbing strike to force your over-controlling brain to shut down, go into a dark room, and stop micromanaging everything.
physiologicalDescription: Intense perfectionism and sympathetic hyper-arousal trigger a wave of cortical spreading depression, accompanied by trigeminal nerve sensitization and the release of vasoactive neuropeptides, causing throbbing cranial vasodilation.
sarcasticAdvice: Superb. By all means, keep trying to force a 100% perfect outcome in every single area of your life. I'm sure that blocking out the sun and vomiting in a dark room is a completely reasonable price to pay for your refusal to delegate or relax.
mindfulnessPrompts: What am I holding on to with such intense, perfectionistic control that my brain has to literally incapacitate me to get me to rest?
Where in my life am I punishing myself for being a normal, flawed human being?
physicalTherapyTip: At the first sign of a migraine, withdraw to a dark, quiet room. Apply an ice pack to the back of your neck and suboccipital area, and practice slow, unhurried diaphragmatic breathing.

name: Nausea
emotionalRoot: Rejection, disgust, overwhelm, swallowed truth, fear, and the sense of being unable to keep something down.
metaphor: Your stomach is an overworked dishwasher named Gary who keeps being handed rotten plates by a restaurant that refuses to close.
physiologicalDescription: Nausea is a warning symptom with many possible causes, including infection, food poisoning, pregnancy, medications, migraine, motion sickness, reflux, gallbladder disease, dehydration, anxiety, pain, neurologic problems, cardiac issues, toxins, or obstruction.
sarcasticAdvice: Before declaring it emotional, check the obvious: pregnancy risk, food poisoning, meds, migraine, alcohol, dehydration, and the suspicious gas station sandwich you trusted like a fool.
mindfulnessPrompts: What can I not stomach right now?
What am I forcing myself to accept?
What does my body want to reject safely?
What is the difference between emotional disgust and a medical red flag?
physicalTherapyTip: For mild nausea, try small sips of fluids, bland foods if tolerated, fresh air, rest, and slow breathing. Seek care for severe abdominal pain, chest pain, dehydration, blood, black vomit/stool, pregnancy concerns, severe headache, neurologic symptoms, fever, stiff neck, persistent vomiting, or poisoning risk.

name: Osteoporosis
emotionalRoot: Foundation, depletion, lack of support, fear of collapse, aging, receiving, and the need to rebuild structure before it breaks.
metaphor: Your bones are a hollow tree that needs density, load, nutrients, fall prevention, and less pretending that foundations maintain themselves.
physiologicalDescription: Osteoporosis is low bone mass and structural bone deterioration that increases fracture risk, often without symptoms until a fragility fracture. Common fracture sites include hip, spine, and wrist. Risk factors include age, menopause, family history, low body weight, smoking, alcohol, low calcium/vitamin D, inactivity, corticosteroids, thyroid/parathyroid problems, kidney/GI disorders, and some medications.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not wait for a bone to snap before believing bone density is a thing. Skeletons are quiet until they become paperwork.
mindfulnessPrompts: What supports me at the deepest level?
Where am I depleted?
What foundation needs rebuilding medically and emotionally?
What risk factor can I address before fracture?
physicalTherapyTip: Bone health care can include DXA/DEXA scan, calcium/vitamin D review, weight-bearing/resistance exercise, balance training, fall prevention, medication review, and treatment such as bisphosphonates or other medications when appropriate.

name: Overweight (General)
emotionalRoot: Accumulation, protection, stress load, unmet needs, metabolic strain, shame, survival storage, and carrying more than the body or life can comfortably move.
metaphor: Your body is an overstuffed house where some boxes are biology, some are stress, some are meds, some are sleep debt, and some are emotional junk mail nobody asked for.
physiologicalDescription: Overweight and obesity involve excess body fat or weight that may affect health, but BMI is only a screening tool. Causes and contributors can include genetics, environment, food access, sleep, stress, medications, hormones, thyroid disease, PCOS, insulin resistance, pain, disability, mental health, trauma, socioeconomic factors, and energy balance.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not reduce weight to laziness. Also do not pretend physics left the chat. Biology is a group project with awful management.
mindfulnessPrompts: What am I carrying that is not mine?
What medical or environmental factor has been ignored?
Where do I need support instead of shame?
What one box can leave the house today?
physicalTherapyTip: Medical review can include BMI limits, waist circumference, blood pressure, A1c/glucose, lipids, thyroid if indicated, sleep apnea, medications, PCOS/hormones, pain, mood, nutrition, activity, and weight-bias-safe support.

name: Weight Issues (Arms)
emotionalRoot: Accumulation, protection, responsibility, unmet needs, shame, and unreached reaching.
metaphor: Your arms are overstuffed storage shelves for reaching, carrying, aging, muscle loss, overall body fat, and the occasional emotional box nobody labeled.
physiologicalDescription: Weight distribution around the arms can reflect genetics, sex hormones, age, muscle mass, total body fat, visceral/subcutaneous fat, insulin resistance, medications, sleep, stress, menopause/pregnancy history, activity, pain, disability, and medical conditions. Spot reduction is not how fat loss works, because biology enjoys disappointing marketing departments.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not shame one body area like it personally betrayed civilization. Also do not pretend labs, sleep, medications, hormones, and food structure are imaginary.
mindfulnessPrompts: What am I carrying in my arms?
What medical factor needs checking?
Where do I need support instead of shame?
What one box can leave the house today?
physicalTherapyTip: Track sudden changes, swelling, pain, shortness of breath, diabetes symptoms, thyroid symptoms, medications, sleep apnea, PCOS/menopause/pregnancy context, eating disorder symptoms, and overall metabolic markers.

name: Weight Issues (Belly)
emotionalRoot: Accumulation, protection, responsibility, unmet needs, shame, and central protection and metabolic risk.
metaphor: Your belly is a central storage room where visceral fat, insulin resistance, cortisol, inflammation, hormones, sleep debt, food pattern, and protection can all pile boxes.
physiologicalDescription: Weight distribution around the belly can reflect genetics, sex hormones, age, muscle mass, total body fat, visceral/subcutaneous fat, insulin resistance, medications, sleep, stress, menopause/pregnancy history, activity, pain, disability, and medical conditions. Spot reduction is not how fat loss works, because biology enjoys disappointing marketing departments.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not shame one body area like it personally betrayed civilization. Also do not pretend labs, sleep, medications, hormones, and food structure are imaginary.
mindfulnessPrompts: What am I carrying in my belly?
What medical factor needs checking?
Where do I need support instead of shame?
What one box can leave the house today?
physicalTherapyTip: Track sudden changes, swelling, pain, shortness of breath, diabetes symptoms, thyroid symptoms, medications, sleep apnea, PCOS/menopause/pregnancy context, eating disorder symptoms, and overall metabolic markers.

name: Weight Issues (Hips)
emotionalRoot: Accumulation, protection, responsibility, unmet needs, shame, and foundation and responsibility.
metaphor: Your hips are the foundation beams of the house, carrying biology, hormones, movement, responsibility, pregnancy history, menopause shifts, and old load.
physiologicalDescription: Weight distribution around the hips can reflect genetics, sex hormones, age, muscle mass, total body fat, visceral/subcutaneous fat, insulin resistance, medications, sleep, stress, menopause/pregnancy history, activity, pain, disability, and medical conditions. Spot reduction is not how fat loss works, because biology enjoys disappointing marketing departments.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not shame one body area like it personally betrayed civilization. Also do not pretend labs, sleep, medications, hormones, and food structure are imaginary.
mindfulnessPrompts: What am I carrying in my hips?
What medical factor needs checking?
Where do I need support instead of shame?
What one box can leave the house today?
physicalTherapyTip: Track sudden changes, swelling, pain, shortness of breath, diabetes symptoms, thyroid symptoms, medications, sleep apnea, PCOS/menopause/pregnancy context, eating disorder symptoms, and overall metabolic markers.

name: Weight Issues (Thighs)
emotionalRoot: Accumulation, protection, responsibility, unmet needs, shame, and carrying forward.
metaphor: Your thighs are the freight elevators of forward movement, carrying genetics, hormones, training history, fluid, fat distribution, and responsibilities that got weirdly heavy.
physiologicalDescription: Weight distribution around the thighs can reflect genetics, sex hormones, age, muscle mass, total body fat, visceral/subcutaneous fat, insulin resistance, medications, sleep, stress, menopause/pregnancy history, activity, pain, disability, and medical conditions. Spot reduction is not how fat loss works, because biology enjoys disappointing marketing departments.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not shame one body area like it personally betrayed civilization. Also do not pretend labs, sleep, medications, hormones, and food structure are imaginary.
mindfulnessPrompts: What am I carrying in my thighs?
What medical factor needs checking?
Where do I need support instead of shame?
What one box can leave the house today?
physicalTherapyTip: Track sudden changes, swelling, pain, shortness of breath, diabetes symptoms, thyroid symptoms, medications, sleep apnea, PCOS/menopause/pregnancy context, eating disorder symptoms, and overall metabolic markers.

name: Pink Eye (Conjunctivitis)
emotionalRoot: Irritation, unbearable sight, tears, anger at what is seen, and the need for clear vision without ignoring infection risk.
metaphor: Your eye is a pair of goggles filled with sand, and the first job is figuring out whether the sand is viral, bacterial, allergic, irritant, or something worse.
physiologicalDescription: Conjunctivitis is inflammation of the conjunctiva. It may be viral, bacterial, allergic, irritant-related, contact-lens-related, or related to other eye disease. Symptoms can include redness, watering, discharge, itching, burning, crusting, and foreign-body sensation.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not share towels like a medieval eye-plague distributor. Also, contact lenses plus red painful eye equals get checked, not vibes.
mindfulnessPrompts: What is irritating my view?
What eye red flag needs urgent care?
What should I stop touching?
What can I see clearly without forcing?
physicalTherapyTip: Track discharge type, itching, pain, vision change, light sensitivity, contact lens use, trauma, chemical exposure, cold/allergy symptoms, one vs both eyes, and contagious exposure.

name: Plantar Warts
emotionalRoot: Burden, stuck pain, carrying something underfoot, delayed attention, and the ache of walking on a problem that keeps asking to be handled.
metaphor: Your foot has a stone in the shoe: a stubborn HPV tenant on the sole that changes every step until you deal with it.
physiologicalDescription: Plantar warts are HPV-related growths on the soles of the feet. They may look callused, have tiny dark dots, grow inward under walking pressure, spread, hurt with standing/walking, and be confused with corns or calluses.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not carve at your foot like a bored raccoon with a pocketknife, especially if you have diabetes, neuropathy, or circulation problems.
mindfulnessPrompts: What burden am I walking on?
What keeps hurting every step?
What needs podiatry/dermatology instead of denial?
What can I put down?
physicalTherapyTip: Track pain with walking, location, black dots, callus-like thickening, spread, diabetes, neuropathy, circulation problems, immune suppression, and prior treatment attempts.

name: Pneumonia
emotionalRoot: Feeling weary of life, carrying deep, drowning grief and emotional wounds that have never been allowed to heal, and feeling completely suffocated by life's heavy demands.
metaphor: Your lungs literally drowning in their own fluid secretions because your spirit feels too tired and weary of life's struggles to keep drawing fresh breath.
physiologicalDescription: Severe emotional fatigue and chronic stress suppress alveolar macrophage function and depress the central respiratory drive, allowing fluid accumulation and microbial pathogens to colonize the deep lung tissue.
sarcasticAdvice: CRITICAL CHECKPOINT: Always seek immediate medical attention for pneumonia. But once you recover, do ask yourself if your absolute exhaustion and refusal to let yourself rest played a part. Keep running on empty while carrying a lake of unshed tears; I'm sure your alveoli will love the next challenge.
mindfulnessPrompts: Where am I feeling so incredibly weary of life's struggles that my lungs are literally giving up the fight?
Can I give myself absolute permission to stop, collapse in safety, and let myself be fully supported without needing to earn it?
physicalTherapyTip: Always follow your physician's medical instructions. For recovery: practice gentle spirometry or deep, slow breathing exercises, and perform gentle back tapping (percussion) to help mobilize lung fluid.

name: Post-Nasal Drip
emotionalRoot: Holding, swallowing words, uncried grief, chronic irritation, and the need to clear what keeps dripping into the throat.
metaphor: Your nose and throat are a leaky faucet dripping into the sink all day while the homeowner keeps pretending the plumbing noise is a personality.
physiologicalDescription: Post-nasal drip is mucus draining into the back of the throat. It is a symptom, not a diagnosis, and causes can include allergies, sinusitis, viral infections, reflux/GERD, irritants, dry air, medications, hormonal changes, pregnancy, deviated septum, nasal polyps, or other nasal/sinus conditions.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not treat every throat-clearing marathon like allergies. Sometimes the culprit is reflux, sinus disease, smoke, meds, or the nose doing interpretive plumbing.
mindfulnessPrompts: What keeps needing to be cleared?
What physical trigger changed before this started?
What needs ENT/allergy/reflux care instead of guessing?
What can I express instead of swallowing?
physicalTherapyTip: Track duration, mucus color/thickness, cough, throat clearing, hoarseness, fever, facial pain, bloody discharge, reflux symptoms, allergy triggers, smoke/irritants, meds, and sleep disruption.

name: Psoriasis
emotionalRoot: Armoring, boundary stress, grief, worry, chronic self-protection, and the feeling that softness is unsafe.
metaphor: Your skin is a construction site where immune-system foremen keep screaming for more bricks even though the wall is already forty feet thick.
physiologicalDescription: Psoriasis is an immune-mediated inflammatory skin disease involving rapid skin-cell turnover, plaques, scaling, redness, itching, pain, genetics, immune signaling, triggers, and possible joint involvement called psoriatic arthritis.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not treat silvery plaques like proof your body is just being dramatic. Your immune system is running a construction crew with no zoning permit.
mindfulnessPrompts: Where have I built protection that now isolates me?
What part of me thinks softness is dangerous?
What boundary could replace armor?
What medical symptom needs care before metaphor?
physicalTherapyTip: Track plaques, itching, pain, cracks, bleeding, nail changes, joint pain, morning stiffness, swollen fingers/toes, heel/back pain, triggers, stress, infections, medications, and flare pattern. Seek care for severe flares, infection signs, eye symptoms, or possible psoriatic arthritis.

name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
emotionalRoot: Self-attack, inward fire, chronic criticism, immune over-alert, exhaustion, and the need for ceasefire plus serious treatment.
metaphor: Your immune system is a confused border patrol firing at your own joint buildings and then wondering why the city is limping.
physiologicalDescription: Rheumatoid arthritis is a chronic systemic autoimmune inflammatory disease that can cause symmetrical joint pain, swelling, morning stiffness, fatigue, nodules, deformity, and extra-articular risks including cardiovascular, lung, eye, and infection concerns.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not treat RA as spicy arthritis. It is an autoimmune disease, not your joints being dramatic for attention.
mindfulnessPrompts: Where am I attacking myself?
What does peace inside the body need?
What flare needs treatment, not toughness?
What support stops the war?
physicalTherapyTip: Track swollen/tender joints, symmetry, morning stiffness duration, fatigue, fever, weight loss, nodules, rash, eye/lung symptoms, meds, infections, and flare triggers. Rheumatology care matters.

name: Right Side of Body Problems
emotionalRoot: Action, forward movement, expression, frustration, blocked initiative, one-sided strain, and the need to triage before assigning meaning.
metaphor: Your body is a car with the right gas pedal jammed, except sometimes the problem is the pedal, sometimes the engine, and sometimes the appendix is trying to resign violently.
physiologicalDescription: Right-sided body symptoms can come from muscle strain, posture, nerve compression, sciatica, neck/back problems, joint issues, circulation problems, stroke/TIA symptoms, gallbladder disease, liver problems, appendicitis, right kidney issues, right lung problems, shingles, injury, inflammation, or referred pain from internal organs.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not label sudden right-sided weakness, numbness, speech trouble, severe abdominal pain, or chest symptoms as blocked masculine energy. That is how vibes lose to emergency medicine.
mindfulnessPrompts: Is this sudden, severe, or neurologic?
Could this be appendix, gallbladder, kidney, lung, heart, stroke, nerve, or muscle?
What action is blocked symbolically after the body is safe?
What needs evaluation instead of interpretation?
physicalTherapyTip: Track exact location, onset, sudden vs gradual, weakness, numbness, face droop, speech/vision/balance changes, chest pain, shortness of breath, right upper abdominal pain, right lower abdominal pain, fever, vomiting, injury, rash, and neurologic symptoms.

name: Ringworm
emotionalRoot: Boundary takeover, colonization, giving up space, stress vulnerability, and needing to reclaim territory.
metaphor: Your skin is a border checkpoint where fungus planted a tiny flag and started expanding the colony like it pays taxes.
physiologicalDescription: Ringworm, or tinea corporis, is a contagious fungal skin infection that often causes a ring-shaped, scaly, itchy rash. It spreads through skin contact, animals, contaminated objects, or shared surfaces and usually needs antifungal treatment.
sarcasticAdvice: Ringworm is not a worm. It is fungus with branding issues, which is somehow worse because it still needs antifungal treatment.
mindfulnessPrompts: Where have I let something take over my space?
What boundary needs reclaiming?
What keeps expanding because I did not stop it early?
What belongs to me that I need to mark as mine?
physicalTherapyTip: Keep the area clean and dry, avoid sharing towels/clothing, treat with appropriate antifungal medication, and seek care for scalp/nail involvement, worsening, spreading, immune suppression, diabetes, or no improvement.

name: Scabies
emotionalRoot: Invasion, boundary violation, feeling occupied, being used without permission, and needing a real eviction.
metaphor: Your skin is an apartment complex where microscopic squatters moved into unit 4B and started hosting mite brunch without signing a lease.
physiologicalDescription: Scabies is a contagious skin infestation caused by Sarcoptes scabiei mites. It causes intense itching, often worse at night, and rash from immune reaction to mites, eggs, and waste. It requires medical treatment and close-contact management.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not try to defeat scabies with vibes, tea tree folklore, and wishful laundry. The mites did not come to negotiate.
mindfulnessPrompts: Where do I feel invaded?
What needs an actual eviction, not just tolerance?
Who or what has been living off my access?
What boundary needs a lock?
physicalTherapyTip: Scabies needs medical treatment, often prescription scabicide. Close contacts often need treatment, and bedding/clothing/towels require laundering or isolation guidance. Seek care for crusted scabies risk, infection signs, severe itching, worsening symptoms, or treatment failure.

name: Sciatica
emotionalRoot: Blocked path, nerve pressure, fear of standing, stuck movement, and the sharp signal that something downstream is being compressed.
metaphor: Your sciatic nerve is a pinched electrical wire sending angry lightning memos from your back into your leg.
physiologicalDescription: Sciatica describes pain radiating along the sciatic nerve pathway, often from lower back or nerve root irritation/compression. Causes can include herniated disc, spinal stenosis, spondylolisthesis, piriformis/deep gluteal syndrome, degenerative changes, trauma, tumors, infection, or other spine/nerve issues.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not treat leg weakness, groin numbness, or bladder/bowel changes like a yoga inconvenience. That is emergency-room language.
mindfulnessPrompts: What path feels blocked?
What signal is shooting for attention?
What red flag needs care now?
Where can I move safely instead of forcing?
physicalTherapyTip: Track pain path, numbness/tingling, weakness, bowel/bladder changes, saddle numbness, fever, trauma, cancer history, infection risk, sitting tolerance, cough/sneeze worsening, and walking limits.

name: Scoliosis
emotionalRoot: Uneven load, imbalance, asymmetry, carrying more on one side, and the search for center without pretending structure is imaginary.
metaphor: Your spine is a leaning tower that needs measurement, monitoring, support, and fewer emotional bricks stacked on one side.
physiologicalDescription: Scoliosis is a side-to-side spinal curvature, often with vertebral rotation. It may be idiopathic, congenital, neuromuscular, adolescent, or adult degenerative. Severity is monitored by exam and imaging such as Cobb angle.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not try to straighten a structural spinal curve with willpower and inspirational posture memes. The spine has filed for engineering review.
mindfulnessPrompts: What load is uneven?
Where do I need support?
What would center feel like?
What structural care needs follow-through?
physicalTherapyTip: Track curve monitoring, age/growth status, pain, uneven shoulders/hips, rib prominence, breathing symptoms, neurologic symptoms, progression, bracing/surgery history, and physical therapy plan.

name: Seizures
emotionalRoot: Overload, nervous-system instability, fear, loss of control, recovery vulnerability, and the need for medical care before symbolism.
metaphor: Your brain is a power plant hit by an electrical storm, and the first job is keeping the building safe, not interviewing the lightning about its feelings.
physiologicalDescription: A seizure is a sudden uncontrolled electrical disturbance in the brain that can change movement, awareness, behavior, sensation, emotion, or consciousness. Causes can include epilepsy, fever in children, low blood sugar, electrolyte disturbance, infection, head trauma, stroke, brain tumor, withdrawal, medications, toxins, sleep deprivation, or unknown causes. A first seizure needs medical evaluation.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not put anything in someone’s mouth during a seizure. Whoever invented that myth deserves to be haunted by safe first-aid posters.
mindfulnessPrompts: Is this a first seizure or known epilepsy?
Did it last over 5 minutes or repeat without recovery?
What trigger or medical cause needs checking?
What safety plan protects the person before meaning-making?
physicalTherapyTip: Track first seizure vs known epilepsy, duration, recovery, triggers, missed medications, sleep deprivation, fever, head injury, pregnancy, diabetes/low blood sugar, substance withdrawal, focal symptoms, aura, injuries, and whether neurologic care exists.

name: Shoulder Problems
emotionalRoot: Carrying too much, overreaching, grief, burden, frozen reach, and the body asking what weight can finally be set down.
metaphor: Your shoulder is an overloaded construction crane with frayed cables, an overheated pulley, and an operator still saying one more lift.
physiologicalDescription: Shoulder problems can involve rotator cuff tendinopathy/tear, bursitis, impingement, adhesive capsulitis, arthritis, instability, dislocation, fracture, labral injury, neck nerve referral, or inflammatory disease.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not keep overhead-lifting through sharp shoulder pain like you're auditioning for the role of Regretful Crane Operator.
mindfulnessPrompts: What am I carrying?
What reach feels blocked?
What weight can go down?
What shoulder symptom needs care?
physicalTherapyTip: Track trauma, sudden weakness, inability to lift arm, deformity, night pain, overhead pain, stiffness, range loss, neck symptoms, numbness, diabetes, thyroid disease, and frozen-shoulder progression.

name: Shingles
emotionalRoot: Waiting for the other shoe to drop. High-level, chronic fear, tension, and acute emotional distress that has been suppressed for months, creating a burning, raw hypersensitivity to your current life situation.
metaphor: Your nerve endings lighting themselves on fire to match the intense, burning anxiety and terror you are feeling about your immediate future.
physiologicalDescription: Stress-induced downregulation of cellular immunity allows the latent Varicella-Zoster virus residing in the dorsal root ganglia to reactivate, traveling down sensory nerves to may contribute to painful, unilateral vesicular skin eruptions.
sarcasticAdvice: Splendid. Let's spend another six months living in a state of absolute, hyper-vigilant terror about what might go wrong, and wonder why your nerve pathways are literally setting themselves on fire. I'm sure more worrying is the cure.
mindfulnessPrompts: What burning fear, anger, or acute stress am I keeping tightly under wraps?
Can I surrender my hyper-vigilant defense and trust that I am safe enough to let my guard down in this moment?
physicalTherapyTip: Seek immediate medical treatment for antiviral therapy. Keep the affected skin cool, dry, and protected. Practice slow, deep, parasympathetic-inducing breaths to reduce neurogenic pain signaling.

name: Slipped Disk
emotionalRoot: Overload, pressure, limits, structural strain, nerve compression, and the body saying the cushion cannot carry endless weight.
metaphor: Your spine is an overstuffed couch whose cushion has blown out and is now pressing on the wiring.
physiologicalDescription: A herniated disc occurs when disc material bulges or leaks through the outer disc layer and may irritate or compress nearby nerves, causing back/neck pain, radiating arm or leg pain, numbness, tingling, weakness, or sciatica.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not deadlift your identity through a disc flare. The spine has already submitted a complaint.
mindfulnessPrompts: What pressure exceeds capacity?
What limit needs saying?
What nerve sign needs urgent care?
What can I stop carrying?
physicalTherapyTip: Track pain location, radiating symptoms, numbness/tingling, weakness, bladder/bowel changes, saddle numbness, trauma, fever, cancer/infection risk, lifting triggers, and function limits.

name: Sore Throat
emotionalRoot: Swallowed fire, withheld speech, irritation, infection, reflux, dryness, and the pain of a throat being asked to carry too much.
metaphor: Your throat is a pipe scraped with sandpaper, and the job is figuring out whether the sandpaper is virus, strep, reflux, smoke, dry air, or voice strain.
physiologicalDescription: Sore throat/pharyngitis is pain, scratchiness, or irritation of the throat. Causes can include viral infections, strep throat, flu/COVID/mono, tonsillitis, allergies/post-nasal drip, dry air, smoke/irritants, vocal strain, reflux/GERD, and mouth breathing.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not demand antibiotics for every sore throat like bacteria personally RSVP’d. Viral throats exist. Strep gets tested.
mindfulnessPrompts: What burns to swallow?
What symptom needs testing?
What needs rest or expression?
What irritant can I remove?
physicalTherapyTip: Track fever, cough, runny nose, swollen nodes, white patches, rash, trouble swallowing, drooling, trouble breathing, severe one-sided pain, exposure, reflux, voice strain, and duration.

name: Snoring
emotionalRoot: Airway resistance, blocked receiving, exhaustion, nighttime struggle, and the sound of breath fighting through a narrowed passage.
metaphor: Your airway is a clogged pipe that vibrates all night while the rest of the house tries to sleep and plot legal action.
physiologicalDescription: Snoring is sound from turbulent airflow through a narrowed or relaxed upper airway during sleep. Causes can include nasal congestion, anatomy, sleeping on the back, alcohol/sedatives, weight, aging, enlarged tonsils/adenoids, deviated septum, and obstructive sleep apnea.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not treat loud snoring with gasping like a funny household soundtrack. That can be sleep apnea, not your throat doing jazz.
mindfulnessPrompts: Where is breath meeting resistance?
Do I have sleep apnea warning signs?
What needs opening physically?
Where can I receive rest?
physicalTherapyTip: Track loudness, gasping/choking, witnessed pauses, daytime sleepiness, morning headaches, dry mouth, high blood pressure, weight changes, alcohol/sedatives, nasal congestion, and sleep position.

name: Muscle Spasms
emotionalRoot: Tension, involuntary release, nervous-system overload, pressure, control, and muscles forcing a discharge the mind kept delaying.
metaphor: Your muscles are circuits on an overloaded electrical grid, and the breaker keeps tripping because the system is tired of being run like a haunted factory.
physiologicalDescription: Muscle spasms are involuntary muscle contractions ranging from twitches to painful cramps. Causes can include dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, overuse, fatigue, nerve compression, medication effects, pregnancy, kidney/thyroid/metabolic disorders, neurologic disease, dystonia, myoclonus, infection, tetanus, or functional/psychogenic movement disorders.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not call every spasm stress when your body may be asking for electrolytes, meds review, nerve evaluation, or emergency care. Muscles are dramatic, but not always metaphorical.
mindfulnessPrompts: What tension is firing without permission?
What physical cause needs checking?
What would safe release look like?
What emergency sign must not be ignored?
physicalTherapyTip: Track location, duration, severity, hydration, exercise, medications, weakness, numbness, bladder/bowel changes, fever, jaw stiffness, recent wound, neurologic symptoms, and whether spasms disrupt sleep or daily life.

name: Sterility
emotionalRoot: Longing, grief, uncertainty, waiting, blocked creation, pressure, shame, and the ache of wanting life to arrive on a timeline it refuses to obey.
metaphor: Your reproductive system is a garden that needs soil testing, water, timing, pollen, sunlight, and sometimes a specialist instead of another year of blaming the moon.
physiologicalDescription: Infertility is difficulty conceiving after a period of regular unprotected sex, commonly 12 months or 6 months when age or medical history warrants earlier evaluation. Causes can involve ovulation, sperm, tubes, uterus, endometriosis, age, hormones, PCOS, thyroid/prolactin issues, genetics, medications, lifestyle, or unexplained factors.
sarcasticAdvice: Infertility is not proof the universe filed a complaint against you. It is a medical and emotional problem that deserves testing, not shame.
mindfulnessPrompts: What am I grieving?
What pressure am I carrying that is not mine?
What medical step would reduce uncertainty?
What form of creation still belongs to me, whatever happens?
physicalTherapyTip: Evaluation may involve both partners: ovulation, cycle pattern, ovarian reserve, semen analysis, thyroid/prolactin, tubal/uterine factors, endometriosis, medications, infections, and timing. Seek care sooner with age over 35, irregular cycles, pelvic pain, known endometriosis, recurrent miscarriage, male factor concerns, or prior reproductive issues.

name: Stomach & Intestinal Problems
emotionalRoot: Dread, fear of the new, or feeling completely unable to digest, assimilate, or process incoming life events. Carrying deep, simmering anger at having to swallow situations you find intolerable.
metaphor: Your digestive engine grinding to a halt or burning itself up because you are trying to force-swallow and assimilate a toxic reality your soul absolutely rejects.
physiologicalDescription: Stress-induced sympathetic activation shifts blood flow away from the gut, alters mucosal barrier permeability ('leaky gut'), and disrupts the enteric nervous system, leading to chronic cramping, bloating, and dysbiosis.
sarcasticAdvice: Outstanding. Keep consuming stressful news feeds, toxic relationship dynamics, and high-pressure work assignments while expecting your stomach to digest them perfectly. I'm sure that your gut problems have absolutely nothing to do with what you are swallowing.
mindfulnessPrompts: What situation, relationship, or news am I forcing myself to 'digest' that feels completely unpalatable?
How can I set clear boundaries to stop swallowing toxic material, allowing my digestive core to heal?
physicalTherapyTip: Practice eating in a completely relaxed, screen-free environment. Spend 5 minutes performing slow, diaphragmatic breathing before meals to activate parasympathetic digestion ('rest and digest').

name: Stroke
emotionalRoot: Giving up. Resisting change, rejecting life, and carrying a deep, hidden belief that 'I cannot go on.' Preferring a total physical shutdown to facing a reality that feels completely overwhelming or restrictive.
metaphor: Your cerebral power grid blowing its main transformer to force a complete, catastrophic shutdown which may somatically reflect an unconscious pattern of seeking escape from an unbearable life situation.
physiologicalDescription: Severe, chronic neurovascular stress and hyper-sympathetic activation may interact with cerebral circulation and risk factors for vasospasm, thromboembolism, or hemorrhage.
sarcasticAdvice: CRITICAL: Seek immediate emergency medical care. But once you recover, do ask yourself if your absolute rejection of change and feeling of total defeat played a role. Keep running yourself into a brick wall of stubborn resistance; I'm sure your cerebral arteries will love it.
mindfulnessPrompts: Where in my life am I feeling so completely defeated, trapped, or resistant to change that my spirit wants to pull the emergency brake?
Am I ready to accept help, embrace change, and find a soft, new way of moving forward without needing to fight?
physicalTherapyTip: Always follow expert medical rehabilitation. For prevention and recovery: engage in gentle sensory stimulation, bilateral integration exercises, and daily, quiet meditation to calm cerebral vascular tone.

name: Stuttering
emotionalRoot: Blocked expression, anticipation fear, speech pressure, shame, avoidance, and the need for safe communication without blaming the speaker.
metaphor: Your voice is a car stalling at the intersection: the trip exists, the driver exists, the engine needs support, and honking at it helps exactly nobody.
physiologicalDescription: Stuttering is a speech fluency disorder involving involuntary repetitions, prolongations, blocks, or disruptions in speech flow. It is often developmental with neurologic and genetic components, but can also be neurogenic after stroke/brain injury or rarely psychogenic/acquired. Anxiety and stress can worsen severity but are not the root cause for many people.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not tell someone who stutters to relax, slow down, or spit it out. Congratulations, you have invented anti-help.
mindfulnessPrompts: Where do I need patience, not pressure?
Did this start suddenly or with neurologic symptoms?
What support helps speech feel safer?
What do I still deserve to say?
physicalTherapyTip: Track childhood vs sudden adult onset, speech blocks/repetitions/prolongations, avoidance, anxiety, new neurologic symptoms, head injury, stroke-like signs, medication/substance changes, and impact on work/social life.

name: Tapeworm
emotionalRoot: Being drained, over-giving, poor boundaries, resource leakage, and the feeling that something is feeding on your effort without returning anything.
metaphor: Your intestines are an apartment building where a freeloading tenant moved into unit 4B, eats your groceries, and somehow still complains about the plumbing.
physiologicalDescription: Tapeworm infection is caused by an intestinal parasite and may come from contaminated food, undercooked meat or fish, exposure to eggs/larvae, or poor sanitation. Diagnosis and treatment require medical care.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not try to evict an actual parasite with affirmations and peppermint tea. That is not medicine. That is giving the worm a scented apartment.
mindfulnessPrompts: Who or what has been feeding on my energy without giving anything back?
Where do I let people access resources they did not earn?
What boundary would stop the drain?
What needs an eviction notice, medically or emotionally?
physicalTherapyTip: Tapeworm symptoms require medical evaluation, testing, and antiparasitic treatment when confirmed. Seek care for visible worm segments, unexplained weight loss, abdominal pain, nausea, diarrhea, weakness, neurologic symptoms, seizures, or signs of infection.

name: Tetanus
emotionalRoot: Not a symbolic diagnosis. Tetanus is a bacterial toxin emergency. Pattern work does not belong ahead of emergency care.
metaphor: Your nervous system is a car with the brake lines cut: the gas pedal is stuck, the muscles seize, and the road is ending.
physiologicalDescription: Tetanus is a potentially life-threatening infection caused by Clostridium tetani toxin. It can cause jaw stiffness, painful muscle spasms, trouble swallowing, neck/back/abdominal rigidity, autonomic instability, breathing problems, and death. Prevention is vaccination and proper wound care.
sarcasticAdvice: There is no home remedy, no detox, no breathing protocol, and no heroic dirt-wound optimism. Tetanus is hospital territory.
mindfulnessPrompts: Emergency care first.
When was my last tetanus booster?
Does this wound need medical cleaning or a booster/TIG review?
Am I treating an emergency like an inconvenience?
physicalTherapyTip: If tetanus is suspected, seek emergency medical care immediately. Prevention includes vaccination boosters and prompt wound care for dirty, deep, puncture, crush, burn, or animal-bite wounds.

name: Teeth Problems (Bruxism, TMJ, Sensitivity)
emotionalRoot: Clenching, held anger, bitten-back words, jaw tension, pressure, and the body grinding through what the mouth refuses to express.
metaphor: Your jaw is a vise clamped on a stack of unsent messages, and your teeth are the poor hardware getting worn down by the meeting.
physiologicalDescription: Teeth and jaw problems can include bruxism, tooth wear, sensitivity, cracked teeth, enamel erosion, gum recession, TMJ disorders, jaw pain, clicking/locking, headaches, dental infection, cavities, sleep apnea overlap, and bite issues. Bruxism can damage teeth and dental work over time.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not let your molars process your emotional backlog. Teeth are expensive little rocks and they do not regenerate out of guilt.
mindfulnessPrompts: What am I clenching?
What do I need to say instead of grind?
What dental symptom needs care now?
Where can my jaw rest?
physicalTherapyTip: Track grinding/clenching, morning jaw pain, headaches, tooth sensitivity, cracked/worn teeth, clicking/locking, trouble opening, swelling, fever, dental pain, sleep quality, snoring/apnea risk, and night guard use.

name: Tinnitus
emotionalRoot: Unwanted noise, hypervigilance, overstimulation, auditory threat scanning, and the desperate wish for quiet in a nervous system that keeps listening for danger.
metaphor: Your ears are a broken radio stuck between stations: static, buzzing, ringing, and the occasional panic DJ refusing to leave the booth.
physiologicalDescription: Tinnitus is the perception of sound without an external sound source, such as ringing, buzzing, hissing, roaring, or clicking. Causes and contributors can include hearing loss, noise exposure, earwax, ear infection, Ménière’s disease, TMJ disorders, head/neck injury, medications, vascular issues, and neurologic conditions.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not ignore sudden hearing loss or heartbeat-synced tinnitus. The ear is not supposed to become a tiny emergency broadcast system without supervision.
mindfulnessPrompts: What noise needs medical evaluation?
Is this sudden, one-sided, pulsatile, or paired with hearing loss/dizziness?
What sound environment helps me cope?
Where does my nervous system need less threat scanning?
physicalTherapyTip: Track one/both ears, sudden hearing loss, dizziness/vertigo, pulsatile heartbeat rhythm, ear pain/drainage, noise exposure, medications, jaw/TMJ symptoms, head/neck injury, and distress/sleep impact.

name: Throat Problems
emotionalRoot: Swallowed words, vocal strain, grief, worry, refluxed pressure, throat tension, and the need to separate expression from actual ENT red flags.
metaphor: Your throat is a stuck elevator: words, mucus, reflux, tension, and irritation are all trapped between floors while the building owner keeps pressing the same button.
physiologicalDescription: Throat problems can include chronic sore throat, hoarseness, globus sensation, throat clearing, irritation, vocal fatigue, or a lump sensation. Causes can include post-nasal drip, reflux/GERD, allergies, sinus disease, infection, dry air, mouth breathing, smoking/vaping, vocal strain, stress-related muscle tension, vocal nodules/polyps, thyroid/neck masses, esophageal issues, or other ENT causes.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not diagnose every lump-in-throat feeling as trapped emotion. Sometimes it is reflux, sinus drainage, vocal-cord trouble, or a throat demanding an ENT with a tiny camera. Charming little nightmare tube.
mindfulnessPrompts: What am I swallowing that needs expression?
What symptom needs ENT care?
What trigger changed: reflux, allergy, voice strain, smoke, infection?
What can I say without forcing the throat?
physicalTherapyTip: Track duration, hoarseness, pain, swallowing trouble, breathing trouble, coughing blood, reflux symptoms, post-nasal drip, smoking/vaping, voice use, fever, neck mass, weight loss, and whether symptoms last more than 2-3 weeks.

name: Thrush (Vaginal Infection)
emotionalRoot: Overgrowth, depletion, imbalance, sweetness overload, immune strain, and letting something small take over the whole garden.
metaphor: Your body is a garden where Candida stopped being a background plant and started opening franchises.
physiologicalDescription: Thrush/candidiasis is Candida yeast overgrowth that can affect the mouth, vagina, skin folds, nipples, or other areas. Risk factors include antibiotics, diabetes/high blood sugar, immune suppression, steroid inhalers, dentures, dry mouth, pregnancy/hormonal changes, irritation, and some medications.
sarcasticAdvice: Candida is not a mystical swamp demon. It is yeast with opportunity and terrible boundaries.
mindfulnessPrompts: What has grown beyond its proper place?
What terrain has been depleted?
What medical factor needs checking?
What can I restore without attacking myself?
physicalTherapyTip: Track location, white patches/discharge, pain, itching, burning, diabetes symptoms, antibiotics, steroid inhaler use, dentures, dry mouth, pregnancy, immune suppression, recurrence, and treatment response.

name: Thyroid Problems
emotionalRoot: Pace conflict, burnout, rushing, slowing, throat expression, fear of falling behind, and the body struggling to find its own rhythm.
metaphor: Your thyroid is the building thermostat, and the heating/cooling crew is either napping in the basement or sprinting through the halls with a clipboard on fire.
physiologicalDescription: Thyroid problems include hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, thyroiditis, nodules, goiter, autoimmune Hashimoto's or Graves' disease, medication effects, iodine issues, pregnancy/postpartum changes, pituitary issues, or thyroid cancer. Symptoms depend on whether hormone levels are low, high, fluctuating, or structurally abnormal.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not adjust thyroid medication by mood, moon phase, or because a forum avatar named GlandWarrior said so. Labs exist. Use the little civilization tools.
mindfulnessPrompts: Where am I moving faster than my body can handle?
Where am I stuck below my natural pace?
What needs labs instead of self-blame?
What rhythm is actually mine?
physicalTherapyTip: Track fatigue, weight change, heat/cold intolerance, palpitations, tremor, bowel changes, mood, hair/skin changes, neck swelling, eye symptoms, pregnancy/postpartum status, medication timing, and TSH/free T4/free T3 if ordered.

name: Tonsillitis
emotionalRoot: Filtering, protection, swallowed fire, boundaries, immune overload, and the need to reject what does not belong.
metaphor: Your tonsils are an overworked nightclub bouncer trying to screen pathogens at the door while the manager keeps understaffing the place.
physiologicalDescription: Tonsillitis is inflammation of the tonsils, commonly caused by viral infections or bacterial infections such as Group A Strep. Symptoms may include sore throat, swollen/red tonsils, white or yellow patches, fever, swollen lymph nodes, bad breath, painful swallowing, and fatigue. Recurrent or chronic cases may need ENT evaluation.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not demand antibiotics for every tonsil tantrum. Viral tonsillitis exists. Strep gets tested. The throat club needs screening, not random pharmaceutical confetti.
mindfulnessPrompts: What needs to be filtered instead of swallowed?
What symptom needs strep testing?
Where am I letting in what hurts?
What boundary protects the throat and the life around it?
physicalTherapyTip: Track fever, cough/runny nose, tonsil patches, swollen lymph nodes, rash, exposure to strep/mono, trouble swallowing, drooling, breathing trouble, dehydration, recurrent episodes, and antibiotic history.

name: Ulcers
emotionalRoot: Swallowed anger, corrosive stress, rumination, boundary erosion, and the feeling that something keeps eating at you.
metaphor: Your stomach is a chemical plant where Gary the janitor keeps patching acid-burned pipes while management refuses to shut the valve.
physiologicalDescription: Peptic ulcers are sores in the stomach or upper small intestine lining. They are commonly associated with H. pylori infection, NSAID use, acid injury, smoking, alcohol, severe physiologic stress, and protective lining breakdown.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not try to out-breathe black stool or vomiting blood. That is not resilience. That is how people become cautionary pamphlets.
mindfulnessPrompts: What keeps eating at me?
Where am I swallowing something that burns?
What boundary has been eroded too long?
What would it mean to protect my lining, physically and emotionally?
physicalTherapyTip: Ulcer symptoms need medical evaluation, especially if persistent or severe. Seek urgent care for vomiting blood, black/tarry stool, sudden severe abdominal pain, fainting, weakness, trouble breathing, or signs of shock.

name: Vaginitis
emotionalRoot: Irritation, imbalance, ignored signals, boundary stress, shame, and the body asking for attention before the tiny downstairs ecosystem starts filing complaints.
metaphor: Your vaginal ecosystem is a walled garden where lactobacilli, yeast, bacteria, pH, hormones, sex, products, antibiotics, and stress are all arguing over landscaping rights.
physiologicalDescription: Vaginitis is inflammation or irritation of the vagina/vulva. Causes can include bacterial vaginosis, yeast infection, trichomoniasis, STIs, irritants/allergies, low estrogen, antibiotics, diabetes, pregnancy, hormonal changes, immune suppression, or other gynecologic conditions.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not self-treat every discharge like a yeast infection. The vagina is not a vending machine where every button dispenses antifungal cream.
mindfulnessPrompts: What signal have I been ignoring?
What changed before symptoms started?
What needs medical testing instead of guessing?
Where do I need attention without shame?
physicalTherapyTip: Diagnosis matters. Track discharge color/odor, itching, burning, pelvic pain, urinary pain, bleeding, sex pain, STI risk, pregnancy, antibiotics, diabetes symptoms, products, douching, and recurrence.

name: Varicose Veins
emotionalRoot: Stagnation, giving without return, heavy legs, pooled effort, receiving difficulty, and the need for flow back to the heart.
metaphor: Your leg veins are a backed-up river trying to flow uphill with faulty valves and a dam-building committee that needs to be fired.
physiologicalDescription: Varicose veins are enlarged, twisted veins usually caused by weakened vein walls or valves that allow blood to pool. Risk factors include age, pregnancy, family history, prolonged standing/sitting, obesity, prior clots, and hormonal factors.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not ignore a suddenly painful, red, hot, swollen leg and call it bad vibes. Blood clots are not famous for respecting vibes.
mindfulnessPrompts: Where is flow not returning?
What am I giving without receiving?
What heaviness needs actual vascular care?
What can move again?
physicalTherapyTip: Track aching, heaviness, swelling, skin color changes, ulcers, sudden pain/redness/warmth, one-sided swelling, pregnancy, clot history, prolonged standing/sitting, and compression tolerance.

name: Restless Leg Syndrome
emotionalRoot: Restlessness, survival mode, fear of stillness, groundlessness, and the feeling that the body must stay ready to run.
metaphor: Your legs are an overcaffeinated guard dog named Rex who keeps patrolling the yard at 2 AM because a squirrel might be plotting.
physiologicalDescription: Restless Legs Syndrome is a neurological sensorimotor disorder involving an urge to move the legs, uncomfortable sensations, worsening at rest or night, and temporary relief with movement. It can be linked with iron deficiency, dopamine pathways, pregnancy, kidney disease, neuropathy, diabetes, medications, caffeine, alcohol, and sleep disruption.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not start iron pills like a goblin mining operation unless a clinician checks your levels. Iron is medicine, not decorative seasoning.
physicalTherapyTip: Track evening symptoms, sleep disruption, caffeine/alcohol, medications, pregnancy, kidney disease, neuropathy, and iron/ferritin status. Seek care for severe sleep loss, sudden weakness, swelling, severe pain, one-sided symptoms, or new neurologic signs.
mindfulnessPrompts: What part of me does not trust stillness?
What am I trying to run from at night?
What would make my legs believe the ground is safe?
What medical trigger needs checking before metaphor?

name: Trigger Finger
emotionalRoot: Grip, control, stuck release, repetitive demand, anger/frustration held in the hand, and the body forcing release after too much holding.
metaphor: Your finger is a sticky desk drawer on a warped track: it can move, but the glide is catching and the release comes as a snap.
physiologicalDescription: Trigger Finger is stenosing tenosynovitis affecting the flexor tendon. The tendon or sheath/pulley can thicken or narrow, causing catching, locking, pain, stiffness, or snapping. Risk factors can include repetitive gripping, forceful hand use, diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, gout, hypothyroidism, and vibration or manual work.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not keep forcing the finger through the lock like you are negotiating with a stuck vending machine. Tendons are not impressed by stubbornness.
physicalTherapyTip: Track locking, snapping, pain at the base of the finger, morning stiffness, grip/pinch triggers, diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, gout, thyroid disease, work tools, and whether the finger gets stuck bent or straight.
mindfulnessPrompts: What am I gripping too tightly?
Where is release becoming forced instead of chosen?
What repetitive demand needs changing?
What needs medical care before metaphor?

name: Trigger Thumb
emotionalRoot: Grip, control, stuck release, repetitive demand, anger/frustration held in the hand, and the body forcing release after too much holding.
metaphor: Your thumb is a clinging monkey gripping a branch: it can move, but the glide is catching and the release comes as a snap.
physiologicalDescription: Trigger Thumb is stenosing tenosynovitis affecting the flexor pollicis longus tendon. The tendon or sheath/pulley can thicken or narrow, causing catching, locking, pain, stiffness, or snapping. Risk factors can include repetitive gripping, forceful hand use, diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, gout, hypothyroidism, and vibration or manual work.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not keep forcing the thumb through the lock like you are negotiating with a stuck vending machine. Tendons are not impressed by stubbornness.
physicalTherapyTip: Track locking, snapping, pain at the base of the thumb, morning stiffness, grip/pinch triggers, diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, gout, thyroid disease, work tools, and whether the thumb gets stuck bent or straight.
mindfulnessPrompts: What am I gripping too tightly?
Where is release becoming forced instead of chosen?
What repetitive demand needs changing?
What needs medical care before metaphor?

name: Migraines
emotionalRoot: Overwhelm, sensory overload, ignored warning signs, forced shutdown, and the brain demanding retreat.
metaphor: Your brain is a power plant running at 150% capacity while the transformers overheat and the manager keeps approving overtime.
physiologicalDescription: Migraine is a neurologic disorder involving recurrent attacks that may include throbbing head pain, nausea/vomiting, light/sound sensitivity, aura, dizziness, sensory symptoms, prodrome, and postdrome. Treatment can include acute and preventive options.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not call migraine 'just a headache.' That is how people reveal they have never had their skull run a nightclub during a power outage.
physicalTherapyTip: Track aura, nausea, light/sound sensitivity, triggers, hormones, sleep, foods, medications, frequency, disability, neurologic symptoms, and red flags.
mindfulnessPrompts: What pressure needs release?
What red flag needs care?
What can power down?
Where am I overthinking instead of resting?

name: Impacted Wisdom Tooth
emotionalRoot: Blocked emergence, pressure, buried wisdom, delayed action, fear of surfacing, and the practical need to let a dentist look at the trapped tooth instead of negotiating with jaw pain.
metaphor: Your jaw is a traffic-choked intersection, and one wisdom tooth is a trapped car wedged sideways while everyone behind it honks into your skull.
physiologicalDescription: An impacted wisdom tooth is a third molar that fails to fully erupt because it is blocked by gum tissue, bone, tooth angle, or lack of space. It may be asymptomatic or cause pain, swelling, pericoronitis, infection, decay, cysts, damage to nearby teeth, headaches, bad breath, or difficulty opening the mouth.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not wait until your jaw becomes a swollen side quest. Dental infections have terrible manners and worse timing.
physicalTherapyTip: Track jaw pain, gum swelling, bad taste/breath, fever, pus, difficulty opening mouth, trouble swallowing/breathing, headache, adjacent tooth pain, recurrent infections, and whether X-rays have been done.
mindfulnessPrompts: What pressure is trying to emerge?
What dental symptom needs urgent care?
What have I delayed because I hoped it would solve itself?
What needs a clear path instead of more force?

name: Loss of Balance
emotionalRoot: Instability, fear of falling, shaken ground, uncertainty, disorientation, and the need to find support before moving.
metaphor: Your body is a leaning tower whose foundation keeps shifting while the architect insists the floor is probably fine.
physiologicalDescription: Loss of balance, dizziness, or vertigo is a symptom, not a diagnosis. Causes can include BPPV, Ménière’s disease, vestibular neuritis, labyrinthitis, migraine, stroke/TIA, multiple sclerosis, Parkinson’s disease, neuropathy, orthostatic hypotension, arrhythmia, dehydration, anemia, hypoglycemia, medications, anxiety/hyperventilation, neck injury, or cervicogenic dizziness.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not walk around proving you're tough while the room is spinning. Sit down before gravity writes the medical report.
physicalTherapyTip: Track spinning vs faintness vs unsteadiness, onset, duration, triggers, falls, hearing changes, headache, double vision, weakness, numbness, speech trouble, chest pain, palpitations, medications, hydration, blood sugar, and neck injury.
mindfulnessPrompts: What kind of dizziness is this: spinning, faintness, or unsteady ground?
What stroke or heart red flag needs emergency care?
What support keeps me from falling?
Where does my life need more stable footing?

name: Breast Cancer
emotionalRoot: Shock, fear, grief, uncertainty, mortality, medical overwhelm, and the need for real treatment plus human support. Symbolically: caregiving, overgiving, receiving support, and body trust without self-blame.
metaphor: The breast is the nurturing field, and a suspicious lump or change is not a metaphor to solve. It is a signal to evaluate.
physiologicalDescription: Breast cancer is cancer arising in breast tissue. Warning signs can include a new lump, thickening, nipple discharge, nipple inversion, skin dimpling, breast swelling, redness, pain, or abnormal mammogram. Treatment depends on subtype, stage, hormone/HER2 status, genetics, and overall health.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not replace oncology, imaging, biopsy, surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy, hormone therapy, or screening with vibes. Vibes may sit in the waiting room and behave.
physicalTherapyTip: Track symptoms, screening history, family history, diagnosis stage if known, treatment plan, side effects, weight loss, pain, bleeding, fatigue, fever, infection signs, and urgent changes. Follow oncology guidance.

name: Allergies & Hay Fever
emotionalRoot: Hypervigilance, reactivity, boundary overload, feeling invaded, and a system that reacts before checking whether the threat is real.
metaphor: Your immune system is an overzealous security guard macing the mailman because pollen walked past the lobby.
physiologicalDescription: Allergies are immune reactions to triggers such as pollen, dust mites, pet dander, mold, foods, insect stings, or medications. Many involve IgE antibodies, mast cells, basophils, histamine, leukotrienes, and inflammatory mediators causing sneezing, itching, watery eyes, congestion, hives, wheezing, or anaphylaxis.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not call throat swelling a spiritual boundary lesson. That is anaphylaxis-shaped danger, and emergency medicine gets the microphone.
physicalTherapyTip: Track trigger, timing, sneezing, itching, congestion, eye symptoms, hives, wheeze, throat/lip/tongue swelling, dizziness/faintness, GI symptoms, asthma history, epinephrine use, and allergist testing.
mindfulnessPrompts: What am I reacting to physically?
Are there anaphylaxis red flags?
What needs avoidance, medication, testing, or emergency care?
Where am I emotionally treating harmless things as threats?

name: Warts
emotionalRoot: Persistence, stubbornness, old patterns, difficulty releasing, and something that keeps returning after being ignored.
metaphor: Your skin is an apartment building with a persistent tenant who keeps filing appeals and filling the lobby with keratin furniture.
physiologicalDescription: Warts are HPV-related viral skin growths. They can be common, plantar, flat, filiform, or genital. They spread through skin contact, shared surfaces, tiny skin breaks, and autoinoculation, and may persist or recur.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not attack face or genital warts with hardware-store confidence. Some locations require a professional, not a bathroom sorcerer.
mindfulnessPrompts: What keeps returning?
What stubborn pattern refuses to leave?
What am I holding that is not helping me?
What needs proper treatment instead of picking?
physicalTherapyTip: Common warts may respond to salicylic acid or cryotherapy, but avoid self-treatment on face, genitals, or suspicious lesions. Seek care for pain, bleeding, rapid growth, many warts, immune suppression, diabetes, genital warts, or diagnosis uncertainty.

name: Wisdom Tooth Impacted
emotionalRoot: Not giving yourself mental space to create a firm foundation. Feeling crowded, pressured, or rushed by life demands before you have matured or felt ready. Suppressing the urge to 'bite' into your destiny because of an overcrowded schedule.
metaphor: Your deepest, oldest teeth getting jammed in the basement of your jaw because you have filled your life with so much mental clutter and urgent noise that there is literally no room left for your wisdom to emerge.
physiologicalDescription: Chronic mechanical jaw clenching and masseter hypertonicity, driven by high-stress autonomic arousal, constrict the temporal bone and mandibular spaces during developmental or eruptive phases, contributing to space limitations and impacted positioning.
sarcasticAdvice: Marvelous. Keep filling every single second of your day with podcasts, emails, and high-pressure work assignments. I'm sure that jamming your third molars sideways into your jawbone is a brilliant way to demonstrate how 'fully booked' your mental capacity is.
mindfulnessPrompts: Where am I crowding out my own deep wisdom, silence, and natural development in favor of urgent, external noise?
Am I ready to create spaciousness in my life, allowing my authentic growth to emerge without pressure?
physicalTherapyTip: Gently stretch your temporomandibular joint by opening your mouth slowly while keeping your tongue on the roof of your mouth. Apply warm compresses to the jaw angle to release masseter tension.

name: Moles
emotionalRoot: Fixed identity, old markers, permanence, fear of change, and places where life feels stamped into the skin.
metaphor: Your skin is a museum exhibit with permanent markers, but some exhibits need a dermatologist before the curator starts writing poetry about them.
physiologicalDescription: Moles, or nevi, are clusters of pigment-producing cells. Many are benign, but changing, irregular, bleeding, itching, painful, new, or unusual moles need medical/dermatology evaluation to rule out melanoma or other skin cancer.
sarcasticAdvice: Do not psychoanalyze a changing mole while ignoring the ABCDE checklist. Melanoma is not impressed by your archetypes.
mindfulnessPrompts: What feels fixed in me?
What old marker have I mistaken for identity?
What needs medical checking before meaning-making?
Where am I allowed to change?
physicalTherapyTip: Monitor moles for asymmetry, irregular border, color variation, diameter/growth, evolution, bleeding, itching, pain, or an ugly-duckling lesion that looks different from the rest.

name: Martyr Complex
emotionalRoot: Over-giving, guilt, depletion, resentment, transactional worth, fear of being useless, and the need to receive without earning it.
metaphor: You are an overworked donkey carrying everyone else’s luggage while the crowd keeps calling it loyalty because nobody wants to pay baggage fees.
physiologicalDescription: A martyr complex is not a clinical diagnosis. It is a behavioral and psychological pattern involving chronic self-sacrifice, over-giving, guilt around self-care, poor boundaries, resentment, burnout risk, anxiety, depression, and physical stress symptoms. Chronic caregiving or over-functioning without support can worsen exhaustion and health strain.
sarcasticAdvice: Stop confusing exhaustion with virtue. A dead battery is not morally superior to a charged one. It is just less useful and more annoying to jump-start.
mindfulnessPrompts: Where am I saying yes because I am afraid of being useless?
What one bag is not mine?
What would receiving look like without earning it?
Am I safe, or am I running on nothing?
physicalTherapyTip: Track burnout, resentment, guilt when saying no, sleep, mood, anxiety, health symptoms, caregiving load, lack of reciprocity, self-harm thoughts, suicidal thoughts, and whether therapy/support is available.
